title: FWKV: I Built My Own Language Model Architecture From Scratch
summary: FWKV (Feed-forward Weighted Key Value) is a novel recurrent LM architecture I designed and built from the ground up. It replaces attention with a single multiplicative state update — and it's already faster than Transformers at scale.
content:
What Is FWKV?

FWKV — short for Feed-forward Weighted Key Value (or Floored Weighted Key Value, depending on who's asking) — is a language model architecture I proposed on May 13, 2026. It's not a Transformer. It's not a copy of RWKV. It's something I built from scratch based on two simple ideas I wanted to explore.

The Core Idea

The whole architecture rests on two design decisions:

  • FFNN as the primary compute block — fast, parallelizable, and hardware-friendly
  • A floored multiplicative recurrent state as the sole context mechanism — no attention, no KV cache bloat

The state update equation is the heart of the model:

S_t = (S_{t-1} ⊙ W) + k_t ⊙ v_t

At each timestep, the previous state gets decayed by a learned weight matrix W, then updated with the outer product of the current key and value vectors. The crucial constraint: W is floored above zero, so the model can never fully forget. This gives stable, bounded memory without gating complexity.

The Models

The FWKV collection currently has three open models, all Apache 2.0 licensed:

ModelParametersTraining DataLink
FWKV-1-hf28.9MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-50M50.6MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-TinyStories—roneneldan/TinyStoriesHF

All models use the custom fwkv architecture and are fully compatible with Transformers and vLLM.

Speed: Why This Matters

The latency numbers are the real argument for this architecture. Benchmarked on CUDA against a standard Transformer and RWKV across model sizes from 10M to 1B parameters, FWKV sits comfortably between the two — but the gap with Transformers explodes at scale. At 1B parameters, Transformers clock in at ~82 seconds for 1024 tokens. RWKV gets down to ~19 seconds. FWKV lands around ~39 seconds, with the error bars showing variance across runs.

That might look like FWKV is slower than RWKV, and at 1B it is. But FWKV's advantage is in its simplicity: there's no complex gating, no CUDA-specific kernel dependency, and the FFNN backbone is trivially parallelizable. The architecture is meant to be a clean, hackable foundation — not a production inference engine (yet).

Try It

There's a live demo Space where you can prompt the model and see it generate text directly in your browser:

  • FWKV Demo on Hugging Face Spaces
  • FWKV-50M Demo
  • Full FWKV Collection

The architecture is young, but the foundations are solid. More sizes and training runs are coming.

title: From 30M Parameters to #1: My Chess Model Just Took the Top Spot
summary: I was checking the community spaces and noticed that ChessSLM, my tiny 30M parameter chess model, just climbed to the #1 spot on Maxime Labonne's leaderboard with a peak Elo of 1146. Here is how I put it together.
content:
I Hit Number One!

I was just checking the community spaces and noticed some unbelievable news: ChessSLM is officially ranked #1 on the leaderboard! It just climbed to a peak Elo rating of 1146, outperforming a bunch of much larger models.

What is ChessSLM?

I designed ChessSLM as a specialized small language model that plays chess using autoregressive natural language move generation. Instead of evaluating physical board states like traditional engines, it treats chess purely as a language modeling problem.

The model is built on a compact GPT-2 architecture (~30M parameters). I pre-trained it completely from scratch on 100,000 chess games sourced from the mlabonne/chessllm dataset using Standard Algebraic Notation (SAN).

The Up-to-Date Leaderboard

Here is how the top of the field currently looks on the official ChessLLM evaluation space:

RankModelElo Rating
1FlameF0X/ChessSLM1146
2DedeProGames/mini-chennus1115
3EleutherAI/pythia-70m-deduped1106
4nlpguy/smolchess-v21092
5nlpguy/amdchess-v91092
6DedeProGames/dialochess1078
7FlameF0X/ChessSLM-RL1056

Capabilities & Match Performance

ChessSLM proves that highly target-focused training data can let a tiny model punch way above its weight class. In my head-to-head evaluation matches against major LLM baselines, ChessSLM actually managed to successfully split results against absolute giants—including a documented win against Claude (though it did drop a match against Gemini).

Current Limitations

Because it operates strictly as a generative language model without an integrated rules engine, it definitely has some quirks:

  • Illegal Move Hallucinations: The model will occasionally try to play moves that violate chess rules because it generates text based on historical patterns rather than legal validation.
  • No State Verification: There is no concrete board state checking mechanism acting as an anchor. It just reads and writes pure notation history strings.
  • Limited Strategic Depth: While it is highly capable at lower Elo levels, it isn't going to match a dedicated chess engine like Stockfish.

What's Next?

Hitting number one on the board proves this approach has serious legs. Moving forward, I want to experiment with adding move legality filters, integrating board state validation, training on much larger datasets, and setting up reinforcement learning cycles through self-play.

Want to see how it plays? You can play against ChessSLM directly on Hugging Face!

title: Introducing LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 & LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX
summary: Two new fine-tunes of Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking: one distilled from Claude Sonnet 4.6's adaptive reasoning, the other trained on 2M coding examples with chain-of-thought thinking. Compact, open, and surprisingly capable.
content:
Two New Models Just Dropped

Hey! I just pushed two new fine-tunes to Hugging Face and figured I'd write a quick post about what they are, why I made them, and how to use them.

LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 — "Liquid Claude"

This one is a knowledge distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking — specifically the version you interact with on claude.ai, not the API variant — into Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking base via SFT. The training data (sourced from FlameF0X/Claude-Sonnet-4.6) is private for now, but the model itself is fully open under Apache 2.0.

The goal was simple: squeeze some of Claude's reasoning style and chain-of-thought behavior into a tiny 1.2B parameter model that anyone can run locally. It tags reasoning, cot, and distillate for a reason — you'll notice the model tends to think before it answers, which is a direct inheritance from the teacher model.

It racked up 1.7K downloads in its first day, so clearly people were curious!

"Model trained on Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (The claude.ai Claude, not the API Claude). The training data is PRIVATE."

LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX — "Liquid CodeX"

This one takes a different angle. Instead of distilling a general-purpose model, I fine-tuned LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking on the Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking dataset — 2 million coding examples formatted with thinking traces. The result is a small model that's surprisingly solid at code generation and technical reasoning tasks.

Training was done with TRL's SFT trainer, and like its sibling, it's Apache 2.0 licensed and runs with standard Transformers or vLLM.

Benchmark Results

Here is how the models stack up on standard evaluations according to the Intel Low Bit Open LLM Leaderboard:

ModelAverageHellaSwagMMLUPiqaSource
LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.646.7639.5131.9968.77Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard
LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX45.2539.7026.5669.48Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard

Why LFM2.5?

Liquid AI's LFM2.5 architecture is a non-transformer sequence model that punches above its weight class at the 1.2B scale. It's fast, memory-efficient, and the Thinking variant already has reasoning baked in — which makes it a great canvas for distillation experiments like these.

Links

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 on Hugging Face
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX on Hugging Face

Both models are free, open, and ready to run!
